items.prompt: A singly linked list must be reversed in place with O(1) extra space. At the start of each iteration, which pair of references most directly represents the algorithm's two frontiers?
constraints: The list is singly linked and acyclic.

options.text: prev is the head of the reversed prefix; current is the first node of the unreversed suffix.
headValue is the smallest payload; tailValue is the largest payload.
left and right are random-access indices into the list.
A queue contains processed nodes and a stack contains unprocessed nodes.

feedback.reason: Iterative in-place reversal is organized around a reversed prefix frontier and an unreversed suffix frontier.
feedback.details: prev identifies the already reversed chain, while current identifies the next node whose outgoing link will be redirected. A temporary successor reference preserves the rest during each step. Value order is irrelevant, linked lists do not provide intrinsic left/right indexing, and auxiliary containers would violate the O(1)-space constraint.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.head_tail_values: Reversal changes link direction, not the ordering rule of payload values.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.left_right_indices: A singly linked list does not provide direct indexed access to symmetric positions.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.queue_stack: Storing all nodes in containers uses O(n) extra space and is unnecessary for the iterative in-place method.

items.prompt: During iterative reversal, select all statements that must hold immediately before each loop iteration under the standard prev/current algorithm.
constraints: No node is allocated or deleted.
The list contains no cycle before reversal.

options.text: The original head remains the entry pointer of the final list throughout the loop.
current is either null or the first node not yet included in the reversed prefix.
The processed payload values are in sorted order.
Following next from prev visits exactly the already processed nodes in reverse of their original order.

feedback.reason: The loop partitions the original nodes into a reversed prefix reachable from prev and an unreversed suffix reachable from current.
feedback.details: The invariant describes both ownership and reachability. Every processed node appears exactly once behind prev with reversed links, while current preserves the entry to all unprocessed nodes. When current becomes null, the partition is complete and prev is the new head. Payload sorting is unrelated, and the original head becomes the final tail after its link is reversed.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.values_sorted: Reversal does not compare or sort payload values.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.original_head_still_entry: The original head ceases to be the final entry; it becomes the tail in a nonempty list.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.prev_reversed_prefix: Without the processed-prefix invariant, the algorithm has no proof that earlier links are correct.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.current_unprocessed_entry: Without preserving the unprocessed entry, remaining nodes may be lost.

items.prompt: The standard iterative reverse initializes prev = null and current = head, then loops while current is not null. What should it return for an empty list and for a one-node list S -> null?
constraints: The input is an ordinary acyclic singly linked list.
The function returns the new head reference.

options.text: Return null for the empty list and S for the one-node list.
Treat both inputs as invalid because no pair of nodes can be swapped.
Return a new dummy node for the empty list and null for the one-node list.
Return S with S.next = S for the one-node list.

feedback.reason: The invariant already covers both boundaries: no processed nodes yields null, and reversing one null link leaves S as the only node and new head.
feedback.details: For an empty list, current is null initially, so the loop performs zero iterations and prev remains null. For S, the algorithm saves null, sets S.next to prev (null), moves prev to S, and terminates. No swap partner, dummy data node, or self-cycle is needed. The null stopping rule assumes an acyclic input.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.dummy_and_null: Reversal does not require adding a logical node, and the existing single node remains the result.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.error_both: The algorithm is defined for lists of length zero and one without pairwise swapping.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.s_self_cycle: A one-node acyclic list remains S -> null; a self-link changes the structure into a cycle.

items.prompt: A wrapper stores dummy.next = head and asks a helper to reverse the logical data list. The output contract excludes dummy. Which call-and-return boundary is correct?
constraints: dummy is implementation-only and not part of the logical list.
The helper reverses every node reachable from its input pointer.

options.text: Reverse starting at dummy.next, set dummy.next to the returned data head, and return dummy.next.
Copy dummy.value into the original tail before reversing.
Skip dummy.next and begin at head.next so the old head is excluded.
Reverse starting at dummy and return dummy so the sentinel becomes the new data tail.

feedback.reason: The sentinel is outside the logical node set, so the reversal domain begins at dummy.next and the result is returned from that link.
feedback.details: A wrapper sentinel can hold the updated head uniformly, but it does not become one of the nodes whose relative order is reversed. Reversing from dummy would place implementation scaffolding inside the result chain. Beginning at head.next would instead lose the original head from the reversal. A public sentinel-based representation could define a different contract, but that is not the stated one.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.reverse_from_dummy: This reverses an extra implementation node and violates the logical membership contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.copy_dummy_value: Payload copying neither establishes the correct reversal domain nor preserves the data-node contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.drop_original_head: The original head is a logical data node and must become the final tail, not be skipped.

items.prompt: An iterative in-place reverse follows next through every node once and stores prev, current, and nextNode. What are its time and extra-space complexities for n nodes?
constraints: Node allocation and recursion are not used.

feedback.reason: The algorithm performs constant work per sequentially visited node and retains only a fixed number of references.
feedback.details: Every node's outgoing link is read and replaced once, so total work grows linearly with n. The algorithm does not jump to symmetric positions or rescan prefixes. prev, current, and nextNode are constant-size state, giving O(1) extra space. A stack-based or recursive implementation would use additional O(n) call or container space in the worst case.

items.prompt: Before reversal the list is A(value=1) -> B(value=2) -> C(value=3). A correct in-place iterative reverse returns C. Which statement describes the result?
constraints: External code may retain references to A, B, or C.

options.text: The result must contain three newly allocated nodes with values 3, 2, 1.
A becomes C because its payload can be changed to 3.
The result is the same node objects C -> B -> A with their original payloads.
The node chain remains A -> B -> C, but the payloads become 3, 2, 1.

feedback.reason: In-place list reversal changes link direction among the existing nodes; it does not redefine identity or require payload changes.
feedback.details: C, B, and A retain their object identities and values. The new entry pointer is C, each next reference is reversed, and A.next becomes null. Swapping values could reproduce the visible value sequence for some callers, but it violates an identity-sensitive contract and leaves topology unchanged. Newly allocated equal-valued nodes likewise do not satisfy in-place reuse.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.values_swapped_only: Payload reversal is not link reversal and can break aliases or metadata tied to node identity.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.new_equal_nodes: Allocating replacements violates the in-place requirement and loses original identities.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.identity_follows_value: Object identity does not change when a payload field changes.

items.prompt: A loop executes: current.next = prev; prev = current; current = current.next. On input A -> B -> C, why does it fail after the first iteration?
constraints: prev starts null and current starts at A.
No other variable stores the original current.next.

options.text: The only issue is that prev was not initialized to a sentinel node.
current.next was overwritten to prev, so current advances backward instead of to original B and the suffix is lost.
The loop cannot work without an index pointing to C.
A.value is not guaranteed to be less than B.value.

feedback.reason: The assignment destroys the original successor before it is saved, so current.next no longer leads into the unreversed suffix.
feedback.details: Initially current is A and prev is null. After current.next = prev, A.next becomes null; then current = current.next sets current to null, ending the loop with B and C unreachable from the algorithm's state. The repair is to save nextNode = current.next before reversing the link and later advance current = nextNode. Payload order, random indices, and a sentinel do not restore the lost reference.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.values_unsorted: Reversal does not depend on payload ordering.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.needs_random_index: A saved successor reference is sufficient; indexed access is neither available nor required.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prev_should_be_dummy: prev must represent an empty reversed prefix initially, which is correctly null for a logical data list.

items.prompt: In A -> B -> C -> D -> E, nodes B through D are reversed in place. A remains before the segment and E remains after it. Select all external reconnections required after the internal reversal.
constraints: The segment boundaries A, B, D, and E are already known.
The input is acyclic and the segment is nonempty.

options.text: Keep A.next at B because B was the original segment head.
Set A.next to D, the new head of the reversed segment.
Set D.next directly to E, skipping C and B.
Set B.next to E, the suffix after the reversed segment.

feedback.reason: The predecessor connects to the new segment head D, and the old segment head B becomes the tail that reconnects to E.
feedback.details: Internal reversal produces D -> C -> B. The untouched prefix must enter at D, and B must exit to E. Keeping A.next at B would enter the segment at its new tail and miss D and C; setting D.next to E would discard the reversed interior. This is the boundary extension from full-list reversal, where there is no external predecessor and the suffix is null.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.a_to_b: B is no longer the segment entry after reversal; retaining this edge makes D and C unreachable from A.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.d_to_e: D must point to C inside the reversed segment, not skip directly to the suffix.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.a_to_d: Without this connection, the untouched prefix cannot reach the reversed segment's new head.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.b_to_e: Without this connection, the untouched suffix E is disconnected from the result.
